Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 3:
> 
> James,
> 
> We're planning to use the EC library in the lfs userspace binary, so we're going to need it in userspace and kernel, unless we just want to make a copy - and I'm sure you can imagine what I think of making a copy vs adding a few #defines :)

If we actually upstream the client and remove the EC kernel code from the Lustre tree afterwards, what does the userspace build look like? Having a dependency on a kernel source tree to build the userspace tools isn't great.

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> > Patch Set 3:
> > 
> > James,
> > 
> > We're planning to use the EC library in the lfs userspace binary, so we're going to need it in userspace and kernel, unless we just want to make a copy - and I'm sure you can imagine what I think of making a copy vs adding a few #defines :)
> 
> If we actually upstream the client and remove the EC kernel code from the Lustre tree afterwards, what does the userspace build look like? Having a dependency on a kernel source tree to build the userspace tools isn't great.

You make a good point, but perf is built out of the kernel tree, right?  I agree it's not ideal, but...

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> > Patch Set 4:
> > 
> > > Patch Set 3:
> > > 
> > > James,
> > > 
> > > We're planning to use the EC library in the lfs userspace binary, so we're going to need it in userspace and kernel, unless we just want to make a copy - and I'm sure you can imagine what I think of making a copy vs adding a few #defines :)
> > 
> > If we actually upstream the client and remove the EC kernel code from the Lustre tree afterwards, what does the userspace build look like? Having a dependency on a kernel source tree to build the userspace tools isn't great.
> 
> You make a good point, but perf is built out of the kernel tree, right?  I agree it's not ideal, but...

perf is included in the main kernel repo. And there isn't much incentive to use a newer perf against an older kernel. Whereas with Lustre tools, I think many people would want the latest tools - if they were available. Especially with stuff like parallel find being added.

Patch Set 4:

Why not just use The intel library directly for user land? Our kernel code is based on that library.

https://github.com/intel/isa-l

Patch Set 4:

Because I want to test *our code*, not some other code that's similar.

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> Because I want to test *our code*, not some other code that's similar.

We can test the code using the kernel kunit API.

Patch Set 4:

Sorry I thought you said their was a idea to use this code with lfs.

Patch Set 4:

What's your use case? i.e. what feature are you going to add to lfs? And does that require harmony between the userspace and kernel EC code? If not, it's worth considering using a pre-existing library.

Patch Set 4: Code-Review-1

James,

I did say that, what's the conflict between those two things?

Tim,

The idea was to have lfs.c able to recalculate EC parity.

Anyway, Andreas noted this isn't necessarily the library version we'd use anyway - Let's forget about this discussion for now.  I'll leave the patch around rather than abandon it, but don't worry about it.

Abandoned

should use newer/assembly optimized ISA-L from upstream

parents.subject: LU-19074 ec: optimize encode with lookup tables

commit.subject: LU-19074 ec: Add EC library sanity test to test suite
commit.message:
LU-19074 ec: Add EC library sanity test to test suite

Add erasure coding library testing to the Lustre sanity test suite
to validate Reed-Solomon functionality and enable automated testing
of EC algorithms in CI/CD pipelines.

The test validates multiple RAID configurations and demonstrates
core EC operations including matrix generation, encoding, and
recovery algorithms.

Includes unified build system allowing the same EC source files
to work in both kernel and userspace environments, eliminating
code duplication and maintenance overhead.

Test-Parameters: trivial
Signed-off-by: Patrick Farrell <pfarrell@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: Ic90fea40e410b613af3482c303fb6fd95f106512
